museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: March gives Britain a homegrown design landmark: Populous.
museum.curatorNote: March has a strong UK-relevant computer-game anchor.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Populous is released for Amiga
items.body: Bullfrog turns terrain, worshippers and divine intervention into a new kind of strategy toy.

items.title: Peter Molyneux and Bullfrog become names to watch
items.body: A British studio shows that experimental systems can be commercially exciting.

items.title: The god game takes shape
items.body: Players are not a person in a world; they are a force acting on it.

items.title: Amiga and ST owners see ambition rising
items.body: The 16-bit computer scene starts to feel less like upgraded 8-bit play and more like its own future.

items.title: Console history remains regionally distant
items.body: The next console wave is coming, but British bedrooms still mostly belong to computers.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors where the evidence supports them.

items.title: Populous
items.body: Bullfrog's god game lets players shape land and people rather than simply move an avatar.
items.sourceNote: Common references cite March 1989 Amiga release.

items.title: SimCity
items.body: Will Wright and Maxis make the city itself the toy.
items.sourceNote: 1989 is secure; exact first platform/month varies by source, so February is used cautiously.

items.title: Super Mario Land
items.body: Mario shrinks into a strange monochrome launch adventure for Nintendo's new handheld.

items.title: Alleyway
items.body: A clean block-breaking launch title that shows the handheld's simple pick-up logic.

items.title: Tetris
items.body: Nintendo's handheld finds the puzzle game that will define its public identity.

items.title: Golden Axe
items.body: Sega's fantasy brawler gives the side-scrolling beat-'em-up a swords-and-magic form.
items.sourceNote: May 1989 appears in several summaries; mark regional timing cautiously.

items.title: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles
items.body: The turtles become part of the year's licensing and arcade conversation.
items.sourceNote: 1989 is secure; platform and regional dates vary.

items.title: Prince of Persia
items.body: Jordan Mechner's rotoscoped animation makes platform movement look alive.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four machines or technology contexts that explain the month's place in gaming history.

items.title: Game Boy
items.body: Nintendo's monochrome handheld launches in Japan and North America, pairing portability with cartridge play.
items.sourceNote: Japan April 21, North America July 31, Europe follows in 1990.

items.title: Sega Genesis / Mega Drive
items.body: Sega's 16-bit console enters North America in August, while Britain mostly watches from a distance.

items.title: TurboGrafx-16
items.body: NEC and Hudson's PC Engine becomes a North American console challenger.

items.title: UK 8/16-bit computers
items.body: Spectrum, C64, CPC, Amiga and ST remain the machines many British players actually own.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Before online gaming was ordinary, paper, shops and local conversations carried the culture.

items.title: Magazine shelves are still the network
items.body: ACE, Zero, Amiga Format, Crash and Zzap move information faster than most players' modems.

items.title: BBS culture remains specialist
items.body: Some PC and enthusiast users know bulletin boards, but most gaming discovery is still local and printed.

items.title: Import knowledge travels as rumour
items.body: Game Boy, Genesis and TurboGrafx stories can be famous before they are locally available.

items.title: The shop window matters
items.body: Screenshots looping on an Amiga or ST can sell a machine as surely as a review.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month as a player might have met it.

items.title: The future became portable
items.body: March 1989 could make games feel smaller, not weaker: a grey handheld, a few batteries, and a cartridge in a pocket.

items.title: Britain was slightly out of phase
items.body: Many of the year's defining hardware moments happened elsewhere first, while UK players lived through micros and magazines.

items.title: 16-bit screenshots had gravity
items.body: Amiga, ST, Genesis and TurboGrafx images made the next decade feel glossy before it was affordable.

items.title: The arcade was still physical theatre
items.body: Golden Axe and Final Fight belonged to cabinets, friends and noise before they became home conversions.
